scenario.title: HIPAA agreement readiness
scenario.stem: A healthcare analytics company is moving a regulated workload to AWS and the compliance team needs to review HIPAA-related agreements and confirm which AWS accounts are covered before protected health information is processed. The team does not want a manual ticket workflow and needs a self-service source of truth for agreements. Which AWS service should they use?

options.label: AWS Artifact
options.rationale: It is the service for reviewing and managing AWS agreements and compliance documents.

options.label: AWS Security Hub
options.rationale: It centralizes findings and compliance posture, but it does not manage AWS agreements.

options.label: Amazon Macie
options.rationale: It helps discover sensitive data in S3, but it does not provide agreement acceptance workflows.

options.label: AWS Trusted Advisor
options.rationale: It provides recommendations and checks, not legal agreement management.

answer.explanation: AWS Artifact is the best fit because it allows teams to review and manage agreements such as HIPAA BAAs and confirm account or organization coverage before handling PHI.
why_others_fail: Security Hub reports findings, not agreement status.
Trusted Advisor provides best-practice guidance, not compliance agreement workflows.

sources.snippet_markdown: You can use AWS Artifact to review and manage agreements for your AWS account or organization. Organizations can accept agreements such as a BAA on behalf of all member accounts.

quality.distractor_rule: All distractors are real AWS security services that are adjacent to compliance but do not manage agreements.
anti_patterns_checked: all distractors are plausible but fail one requirement

scenario.title: Cross-service troubleshooting logs
scenario.stem: A retail platform is troubleshooting intermittent checkout failures across application components and AWS services. Engineers want one place to store, search, and analyze logs instead of pulling data from multiple systems during each incident. The operations lead wants centralized log visibility without building a custom pipeline first. Which AWS service best fits?

options.label: AWS CloudTrail
options.rationale: It records API activity, but it is not the general-purpose centralized application log service described here.

options.label: Amazon CloudWatch Logs
options.rationale: It centralizes, stores, searches, and analyzes logs from applications and AWS services.

options.label: AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS)
options.rationale: It manages encryption keys, not operational log storage.

options.label: Amazon Inspector
options.rationale: It scans for vulnerabilities, not centralized log troubleshooting.

answer.explanation: Amazon CloudWatch Logs is the best fit because it centralizes logs from systems, applications, and AWS services so engineers can search and analyze them during incidents.
why_others_fail: CloudTrail focuses on AWS API activity rather than general application and service logs.
KMS manages keys rather than log ingestion and analysis.
Inspector scans workloads but does not act as a log platform.

sources.snippet_markdown: CloudWatch Logs enables you to centralize logs from systems, applications, and AWS services in a single, highly scalable service. Teams can then view, search, filter, archive, and analyze log data with CloudWatch Logs Insights.

quality.distractor_rule: All distractors are real AWS services commonly confused with logging or security operations but fail the central-log requirement.
anti_patterns_checked: all distractors are plausible but fail one requirement
